perturbation.original_text: The prevailing party in any action to enforce this Agreement shall be entitled to reasonable costs and attorneys’ fees.
perturbation.changed_text: The prevailing party in any action to enforce this Agreement shall be entitled to costs, but not attorneys’ fees.
perturbation.explanation: In California, some statutes allow for attorney's fees to the prevailing party in contract disputes; limiting this removes a possible right.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1717 allows for the recovery of attorney's fees in contract actions where the contract provides for it. By explicitly disallowing attorney's fees, the modified text contradicts this statute by preventing the prevailing party from recovering such fees, potentially limiting their legal recourse and discouraging them from enforcing their contractual rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1717 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1717. (a) In any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney’s fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce that contract, shall be awarded either to one of the parties or to the prevailing party, then the party who is determined to be the party prevailing on the contract, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs. Where a contract provides for attorney’s fees, as set forth above, that provision shall be construed as applying to the entire contract, unless each party was represented by counsel in the negotiation and execution of the contract, and the fact of that representation is specified in the contract. Reasonable attorney’s fees shall be fixed by the court, and shall be an element of the costs of suit. Attorney’s fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract which is entered into after the effective date of this section. Any provision in any such contract which provides for a waiver of attorney’s f
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly removes the provision for attorneys' fees, while the original text allows for 'reasonable costs and attorneys’ fees.' According to California Civil Code Section 1717(a), 'In any action on a contract, where the contract specifically provides that attorney’s fees and costs...shall be awarded...to the prevailing party...shall be entitled to reasonable attorney’s fees in addition to other costs.' By disallowing attorney's fees in the modified version, the contract directly contradicts the statute, which dictates that if a contract provides for attorney's fees, the prevailing party is entitled to them. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists as the changed text removes a right that is protected by law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the law of the State of California without regard to the conflicts of law provisions thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the law of Canada without regard to the conflicts of law provisions thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from California to Canada may remove consumer protections afforded under California law, particularly if the agreement is executed or performed in California.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Consumer Legal Remedies Act (CLRA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 1750 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CLRA provides specific remedies and protections to consumers in California. By changing the governing law to Canada, the modified text potentially deprives California consumers of these protections, contradicting the intent and application of the CLRA if the transaction occurs within California.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.5. CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1750 - 1756] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1750. This title may be cited as the Consumers Legal Remedies Act. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from California to Canada. The Law Explanation indicates that this change could deprive California consumers of protections under the CLRA if the transaction occurs within California. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the Consumers Legal Remedies Act (CLRA) as CIV 1750, suggesting that California law provides specific consumer protections that are potentially nullified by applying Canadian law. Since the original contract specified California law to govern the agreement and this has now been changed, a contradiction arises as legally mandated consumer protections in California could be omitted.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement supersedes all prior discussions and writings and constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement supersedes all prior discussions and writings and constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to some of the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'some of' creates ambiguity about whether all relevant prior agreements are superseded. This could open the door to arguments that certain consumer rights or protections established in earlier communications or agreements still apply, even if they conflict with the current agreement, potentially violating the principle of clear and complete agreement in contract law and opening the door to legal challenges.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1856 addresses the parol evidence rule, which generally states that the terms of a written agreement intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement cannot be contradicted by evidence of any prior agreement. By limiting the superseding effect to 'some of' the subject matter, the modified text creates ambiguity and weakens the integration clause, potentially allowing prior discussions and agreements to be admitted as evidence to contradict the written terms, increasing the risk of misinterpretation and legal disputes.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1856 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1856. (a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (f
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by limiting the superseding effect to 'some of' the subject matter, introduces ambiguity regarding which prior agreements are still valid. This directly contradicts the principle outlined in California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1856(a), which states that terms in a final written agreement 'may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement'. The modification weakens the integration clause, increasing the risk that prior discussions and agreements could be admitted to contradict the written terms, leading to legal disputes.
